pageTitle: History of the Global LEI System

author: No author specified
content: In the wake of the 2008 financial crisis, regulators worldwide acknowledged their inability to identify parties to transactions across markets, products, and regions. The Financial Stability Board (FSB), together with the finance ministers and central bank governors represented in the Group of 20 (G20), therefore, advocated developing a universal Legal Entity Identifier (LEI) applicable to any legal entity that engages in financial transactions.
pageTitle: History of the Global LEI System
firstHeadingBeforeElement: History of the Global LEI System

author: No author specified
content: Implementation of the LEI would increase the authorities’ ability to evaluate systemic and emerging risk, identify trends, and take corrective steps.
pageTitle: History of the Global LEI System
firstHeadingBeforeElement: History of the Global LEI System

author: No author specified
content: At its November 2011 Cannes Summit, the G20 called upon the FSB to take the lead in developing recommendations for a global LEI and supporting governance structure.
pageTitle: History of the Global LEI System
firstHeadingBeforeElement: History of the Global LEI System

author: No author specified
content: The resulting FSB recommendations were subsequently endorsed by the G20 at its 2012 Los Cabos summit. The ‘G20 Leaders Declaration’ of 19 June 2012 states: “We endorse the FSB recommendations regarding the framework for development of a global LEI system for parties to financial transactions, with a global governance framework representing the public interest. (...) We encourage global adoption of the LEI to support authorities and market participants in identifying and managing financial risks.”
pageTitle: History of the Global LEI System
firstHeadingBeforeElement: History of the Global LEI System

author: No author specified
content: Following up on the FSB's recommendations, authorities, working with the private sector, developed a framework for a Global LEI System that provides, through the issuance of LEIs, unique identification of legal entities participating in transactions and other official interactions across the globe.
pageTitle: History of the Global LEI System
firstHeadingBeforeElement: History of the Global LEI System

author: No author specified
content: In November 2012, the G20 finance ministers and central bank governors, along with the FSB, endorsed the Charter of the Regulatory Oversight Committee (ROC). The ROC was established in January 2013.
pageTitle: History of the Global LEI System
firstHeadingBeforeElement: History of the Global LEI System

author: No author specified
content: In January 2014, the FSB Plenary, in its capacity as the founder of the Global Legal Entity Identifier Foundation (GLEIF), endorsed nominees to the initial GLEIF Board of Directors. These nominees were recommended to the FSB by the ROC. GLEIF is a not-for-profit organization created to support the implementation and use of the LEI.
pageTitle: History of the Global LEI System
firstHeadingBeforeElement: History of the Global LEI System

author: No author specified
content: The GLEIF Board held its inaugural meeting in Zurich, Switzerland, on 26 June 2014. The Board of Directors subsequently announced the appointment of Stephan Wolf as GLEIF Chief Executive Officer.
pageTitle: History of the Global LEI System
firstHeadingBeforeElement: History of the Global LEI System

author: No author specified
content: On June 26, 2024, Alexandre Kech took over from Stephan Wolf as CEO of GLEIF.
pageTitle: History of the Global LEI System
firstHeadingBeforeElement: History of the Global LEI System

author: No author specified
content: The FSB emphasized that global adoption of the LEI underpins multiple “financial stability objectives” and offers “many benefits to the private sector”.
pageTitle: History of the Global LEI System
firstHeadingBeforeElement: History of the Global LEI System

author: No author specified
content: In keeping with these sentiments, GLEIF now envisions a future where every business has a verifiable, trusted global identity – the LEI – hardwired into all relationships and transactions.
pageTitle: History of the Global LEI System
firstHeadingBeforeElement: History of the Global LEI System

author: No author specified
content: To enable businesses to reap the many benefits offered by their participation in the global digital economy, GLEIF has developed the verifiable LEI (vLEI), the digital counterpart to the LEI, and expanded the Global LEI System to incorporate vLEI Issuers, also referred to as Qualified vLEI Issuers.
pageTitle: History of the Global LEI System
firstHeadingBeforeElement: History of the Global LEI System

author: No author specified
content: By making both the LEI and vLEI available, GLEIF and the Global LEI System provide a broad ‘public good’ by building on local authoritative sources and registries to extend access to trusted information globally, supporting integrity and transparency across the digital economy.
pageTitle: History of the Global LEI System
firstHeadingBeforeElement: History of the Global LEI System

author: No author specified
content: In addition to being an internationally recognized organizational identity management infrastructure, today’s Global LEI System also serves as a global Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI).
pageTitle: History of the Global LEI System
firstHeadingBeforeElement: History of the Global LEI System

author: No author specified
content: As a global DPI, the Global LEI System provides organizational identifiers that support and extend the interoperability of local and national infrastructures (e.g., business registries), enabling an internationally recognized, freely available business discovery and verification protocol. It helps infrastructures, businesses, and entities participate in the global digital economy. Through the Global LEI System, local infrastructures can make trusted business information more accessible and verifiable globally without sacrificing integrity or control.
pageTitle: History of the Global LEI System
firstHeadingBeforeElement: History of the Global LEI System

author: No author specified
content: Verified organizational identity is a prerequisite for an efficient, scalable trust ecosystem that lowers transaction costs and risk while fostering collaboration, participation, and innovation. It enables money, goods, and services to flow more freely and securely around the world.
pageTitle: History of the Global LEI System
firstHeadingBeforeElement: History of the Global LEI System

author: No author specified
content: Hardwiring Trust into All Business Relationships
pageTitle: Hardwiring Trust into All Business Relationships

author: No author specified
content: All business relationships depend on trust.
pageTitle: Hardwiring Trust into All Business Relationships
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Hardwiring Trust into All Business Relationships

author: No author specified
content: Is this a legitimate company? What identifying data is available? How has it been verified? Are its authorized representatives who they claim to be?
pageTitle: Hardwiring Trust into All Business Relationships
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Hardwiring Trust into All Business Relationships

author: No author specified
content: Digital technologies have enabled an explosion in global commerce. More companies than ever before are now doing business across borders, languages, and legal jurisdictions. Inevitably, this has made compliance and due diligence requirements harder to meet, from locating a partner’s business registration data to signing contracts remotely, authorizing cross-border payments, and even authenticating key people involved in official company interactions. The lack of interoperability between enabling technologies, together with the pace of change, compounds the challenge.
pageTitle: Hardwiring Trust into All Business Relationships
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Hardwiring Trust into All Business Relationships

author: No author specified
content: This is where organizational identity takes centre stage.
pageTitle: Hardwiring Trust into All Business Relationships
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Hardwiring Trust into All Business Relationships

author: No author specified
content: The Global Legal Entity Identifier Foundation (GLEIF) maintains the Global LEI System, an internationally recognized organizational identity management infrastructure and a global Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI). The Global LEI System is open, based on ISO standards, and governed by a globally representative oversight committee. It provides a trusted, regulator-endorsed, publicly accessible infrastructure for enabling secure digital identity, data exchange, and financial system connectivity.
pageTitle: Hardwiring Trust into All Business Relationships
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Hardwiring Trust into All Business Relationships

author: No author specified
content: The Legal Entity Identifier (LEI) and Global LEI System provide a broad ‘public good’ by building on local authoritative sources and registries to extend access to trusted information globally, supporting integrity and transparency across the digital economy.
pageTitle: Hardwiring Trust into All Business Relationships
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Hardwiring Trust into All Business Relationships

author: No author specified
content: Together, the LEI and its digital counterpart, the verifiable LEI (vLEI), empower organizations with verified identity tools that enable:
pageTitle: Hardwiring Trust into All Business Relationships

pageTitle: Hardwiring Trust into All Business Relationships

content: Autonomy to access and manage their own credentials and data, creating greater opportunities to participate in the formal economy.
pageTitle: Hardwiring Trust into All Business Relationships

author: No author specified
content: Participation in the evolving digital financial and payments ecosystems.
pageTitle: Hardwiring Trust into All Business Relationships

author: No author specified
content: Compliance and security in a world in which regulators and industry are increasingly calling for greater openness, accountability, and control in a data-driven, global digital marketplace.

author: No author specified
content: Call for Papers: The Digital Policy Trilemma
pageTitle: Call for Papers: The Digital Policy Trilemma

author: No author specified
content: Digital organizational identity is increasingly central to a wide range of policy objectives – from modernizing public services and enabling safe financial innovation to strengthening digital trade and cross-border activity. It also plays a key role in managing risks such as fraud, cybercrime and identity misuse, while supporting data protection, privacy and emerging use cases such as agentic AI and verifiable data sharing. At the same time, policymakers and market participants face a key challenge: how to design digital identity systems that balance global interoperability with diverse policy priorities and innovation goals.
pageTitle: Call for Papers: The Digital Policy Trilemma

author: No author specified
content: To address these questions, GLEIF invites experts in digital public policy, economics, law and technology to submit proposals contributing to the future design of digital organizational identity ecosystems.
pageTitle: Call for Papers: The Digital Policy Trilemma

author: No author specified
content: Call for Papers: “Aligning policy aims for international Digital Organizational Identity infrastructure – is there a digital policy trilemma?”
pageTitle: Call for Papers: The Digital Policy Trilemma

author: No author specified
content: This Call for Papers invites academics, policymakers and practitioners in digital public policy, economics, law and technology to submit proposals for papers that will inform policy makers, regulators and digital agencies on how to design standards, technology and governance for digital organizational identity and related downstream markets across financial systems, public sector use cases and the broader digital economy.
pageTitle: Call for Papers: The Digital Policy Trilemma

author: No author specified
content: GLEIF is particularly interested in interdisciplinary contributions that connect:
pageTitle: Call for Papers: The Digital Policy Trilemma

content: policy and regulation
pageTitle: Call for Papers: The Digital Policy Trilemma

author: No author specified
content: economic and market design
pageTitle: Call for Papers: The Digital Policy Trilemma

author: No author specified
content: technology and data standards
pageTitle: Call for Papers: The Digital Policy Trilemma

author: No author specified
content: Submissions should provide practical, evidence-based insights that can inform the development of efficient, trusted and globally interoperable identity ecosystems.
pageTitle: Call for Papers: The Digital Policy Trilemma

pageTitle: Call for Papers: The Digital Policy Trilemma

content: Opportunity to connect with policy makers, academics and practitioners around the world working on digital identities to bring forward ideas and influence the debate on aligning policy aims
pageTitle: Call for Papers: The Digital Policy Trilemma

author: No author specified
content: Recognition through awards and review by an international panel of experts; Authors of the ‘best paper’ will be awarded a total prize of EUR 5K; two runners-ups will each receive a prize of EUR 2.5K
pageTitle: Call for Papers: The Digital Policy Trilemma

author: No author specified
content: International experts will review submissions
pageTitle: Call for Papers: The Digital Policy Trilemma

author: No author specified
content: The Reviewer of proposals and papers consists of Professor Greg Taylor (Oxford), Dr David Porteous (Integral Governance Solutions), Professor Alexandre de Cornière (Toulouse School of Economics), Professor James Lei (The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (HKUST)), Professor Sean Ennis (University of East Anglia), Benedicte Nolens (BIS Innovation Hub, Hong Kong), Sophia Hasnain (CEO Linked Things ), Goran Vranic (World Bank) and Pavle Avarmovic (Financial Innovation for Impact and Cambridge Centre for Alternative Finance)
pageTitle: Call for Papers: The Digital Policy Trilemma

author: No author specified
content: LEI & vLEI

author: No author specified
content: Global Legal Entity Identifier Foundation (GLEIF) envisions a future where every business has a verifiable trusted global identity – the Legal Entity Identifier (LEI) – hardwired into all relationships and transactions.

author: No author specified
content: For individual businesses, this would unlock almost limitless opportunities for them to participate in global markets.

author: No author specified
content: Globally, it would ease international trade, accelerate economic growth, and help to address a range of global challenges: financial and corporate fraud, financial exclusion, friction in international capital flows, the burden of environmental compliance, money laundering, and the financing of terrorism.

author: No author specified
content: Fuelled by digital transformation, levels of cooperation and competition between businesses are rapidly increasing worldwide. To enable this growth to continue, GLEIF has developed the verifiable LEI (vLEI), the digital counterpart to the LEI.

author: No author specified
content: The vLEI enables businesses to leverage the trust associated with their LEI across their digitized business processes and transactions.

author: No author specified
content: It creates digitized trust on a global scale.

author: No author specified
content: The Legal Entity Identifier (LEI)

author: No author specified
content: The birthplace of global organizational identity

author: No author specified
content: The Legal Entity Identifier (LEI) is the starting point for achieving a globally connected network of verifiable, interoperable business identity and information. It is a unique 20-character alphanumeric code that enables anyone, anywhere in the world, to access clear, unique identification data about a legal entity.
firstHeadingBeforeElement: The birthplace of global organizational identity

author: No author specified
imgMeta: The birthplace of global organizational identity The Legal Entity Identifier (LEI) is the starting point for achieving a globally connected network of verifiable, interoperable business identity and information. It is a unique 20-character alphanumeric code that enables anyone, anywhere in the world, to access clear, unique identification data about a legal entity.   GLEIF's Legal Entity Identifier (LEI): 506700GE1G29325QX363 Every LEI is unique and can represent only one entity. Each entity’s LEI code connects to verified reference information from authoritative local sources, including the entity’s ownership structure, which is accessible in the Global LEI Index. In this way, the LEI answers the questions of 'who is who’ and ‘who owns whom’. LEI adheres to four necessary principles of ISO 17442:

author: No author specified
content: Download as PDF: The Legal Entity Identifier - Questions and Answers

author: No author specified
content: The LEI in Digital Certificates

author: No author specified
content: The updated ISO 17442-2 standard, released in 2020, enables trust service providers to consistently embed Legal Entity Identifiers (LEIs) in digital certificates.

author: No author specified
content: Since the LEI is a global, secure mechanism that provides reliable organizational identity data, incorporating LEIs into digital certificates and document e-signature processes provides a powerful, additional layer of trust that supports digital transformation.

author: No author specified
content: An embedded LEI code can be checked consistently and automatically, regardless of the entity’s location, in the same way that the validation of the host certificate is performed. It also provides a direct link to that entity’s LEI-verified reference data, enabling automated revocation monitoring. The net result?  Strong and reliable validation of an organization’s current data, together with the identity of those acting on behalf of the company.

author: No author specified
content: Keen to get the LEI embedded in your eSeal or digital certificate? Get in touch with us at info@gleif.org.

author: No author specified
content: The verifiable LEI (vLEI)

author: No author specified
content: Digital organizational identity for everyone

author: No author specified
content: The verifiable Legal Entity Identifier (vLEI) is a decentralized digital identity that enables counterparties to computationally verify the identity, authority, and role of people acting on behalf of any legal entity. In 2024, a third update to the LEI standard, ISO 17442-3, defined and standardized the use of vLEIs as digital credentials across the global LEI ecosystem.

author: No author specified
content: The vLEI provides instant, automated identity verification of legal entities and those acting on the organization’s behalf. As a verifiable credential, it represents the next generation of identity management and the natural evolution of identity credentials. It ensures robust organizational identity as providers move beyond widely used digital certificates to verifiable credentials issued to specific persons in specific roles. The vLEI is a secure, user-controlled, digitally trustworthy counterpart to an entity’s LEI. It can be used in any kind of digital interaction, transaction, or e-signature scenario, from signing regulatory filings and reports to authorizing business payments and expediting supply chain due diligence.

author: No author specified
content: The vLEI cryptographically combines three identity sources: the entity’s identity, confirmed by their LEI, an individual’s personal identity, and the official organizational role they hold.

author: No author specified
imgMeta: e, user-controlled, digitally trustworthy counterpart to an entity’s LEI. It can be used in any kind of digital interaction, transaction, or e-signature scenario, from signing regulatory filings and reports to authorizing business payments and expediting supply chain due diligence. The vLEI cryptographically combines three identity sources: the entity’s identity, confirmed by their LEI, an individual’s personal identity, and the official organizational role they hold.   Embedding the LEI in digital tools - Representing Organizations, Persons and Roles Because the vLEI leverages the well-established Global LEI System, the only open, standardized, and regulatory-endorsed legal entity identification system, it is uniquely capable of establishing digital trust between all organizations, everywhere. The vLEI issuance system establishes GLEIF as the digital ‘root of trust’. This safeguards the integrity of the vLEI digital trust chain and means all vLEIs are openly traceable, through a cryptographically protected chain of credentials, back to their identity data source - their LEI record in the Global LEI Index.

author: No author specified
content: The Global LEI Index

author: No author specified
content: High-quality data, easily accessible

author: No author specified
content: Global Legal Entity Identifier Foundation (GLEIF) makes all Legal Entity Identifier (LEI) records publicly available in the Global LEI Index, a data-rich global directory that is open and free to access by anyone, anywhere in the world.

author: No author specified
content: Since its inception in 2014, the Global LEI Index has grown to more than three million LEI records, greatly enhancing transparency in the global marketplace, particularly in financial markets where the LEI is widely mandated. The Global LEI Index is routinely accessed by a wide range of data consumers, from regulatory supervisors to business data service providers and individual legal entities.

author: No author specified
content: In line with the principles of ISO 17442, GLEIF’s commitment to maintaining both high-quality data and universal, no-cost access informs how it develops and administers the Global LEI Index.

author: No author specified
content: Data quality

author: No author specified
content: GLEIF is committed to best practice in data quality. It maintains a stringent data quality management system that benefits data governance and compliance at an individual entity level. This also provides data confidence for supervisors and a solid foundation for trusted open data initiatives.

author: No author specified
content: Data access

author: No author specified
content: GLEIF is engaged in a plethora of data governance initiatives and sector-specific technical integration partnerships to increase the capacity of the Global LEI System for the public good. It also runs its own digital transformation programs for the same purpose.

author: No author specified
content: To ease data access, for example, GLEIF has developed integration APIs, machine learning tools, and advanced search facilities.

author: No author specified
content: With a view to increasing the utility of LEI data, GLEIF has engaged in a variety of open data initiatives, including mapping partnerships with complementary identifiers and datasets, and linking projects to business registries.

author: No author specified
content: Find out more about the Global Open Data Integration Network (GODIN), launched by GLEIF and Open Ownership to strengthen open data interoperability and transparency across sectors.

author: No author specified
content: Get an LEI/vLEI

author: No author specified
content: Together, the Legal Entity Identifier (LEI) and verifiable LEI (vLEI) represent a powerful union that provides a trusted global identity to organizations as well as their organizational roles.

author: No author specified
content: Legal entities wishing to obtain LEI / vLEI identity credentials must supply accurate reference data to their issuing organization. Although in some cases this is even simpler. A number of LEI Issuers, also referred to as a Local Operating Units (LOUs), facilitate reference data retrieval from the local registration authority. This means that the legal entity only has to provide its local identity number to start the application process.

author: No author specified
imgMeta: Together, the Legal Entity Identifier (LEI) and verifiable LEI (vLEI) represent a powerful union that provides a trusted global identity to organizations as well as their organizational roles. Legal entities wishing to obtain LEI / vLEI identity credentials must supply accurate reference data to their issuing organization. Although in some cases this is even simpler. A number of LEI Issuers, also referred to as a Local Operating Units (LOUs), facilitate reference data retrieval from the local registration authority. This means that the legal entity only has to provide its local identity number to start the application process.   The reference data includes: te parents of a legal entity, if applicable. This information is referred to as ‘Level 2’ data. It answers the question of ‘who owns whom’.

author: No author specified
content: The reference data includes:

author: No author specified
content: Business card information, e.g. the official name of a legal entity and its registered address. In the Global LEI System, this information is referred to as ‘Level 1’ data. It provides the answer to the question of ‘who is who’.

author: No author specified
content: Relationship information, which allows the identification of the direct and ultimate parents of a legal entity, if applicable. This information is referred to as ‘Level 2’ data. It answers the question of ‘who owns whom’.

author: No author specified
content: The Global LEI System provides a variety of options for entities wishing to obtain their LEI and vLEI.

author: No author specified
content: Obtaining an LEI is the first step.

author: No author specified
content: Once an entity has been issued with an LEI, it may then apply for a vLEI.

author: No author specified
content: Get an LEI

author: No author specified
content: 1. Contact an LEI Issuer

author: No author specified
content: LEI Issuers, also referred to as Local Operating Units (LOUs), supply registration, identity verification, renewal, and other services, and act as the primary interface for legal entities wishing to obtain a Legal Entity Identifier (LEI).

author: No author specified
content: Find and contact your preferred LEI Issuer in your jurisdiction using the search and filter facility below. Note: Legal entities are not limited to using an LEI Issuer domiciled in its own country; it can use the registration services of any issuer that is accredited for its authorized jurisdiction(s).

author: No author specified
content: To find out which LEI Issuers provide services for fund entities in a given country, please select the relevant country from the drop-down box and make sure to check the “I want to register a fund” box.

author: No author specified
content: Select one or more jurisdictions to filter LEI issuer

author: No author specified
content: {{ lou_name_current }}

author: No author specified
content: Registration Agents of {{ lou_name_current }}

author: No author specified
content: No Registration Agents available

author: No author specified
content: 2.    Contact a Registration Agent

author: No author specified
content: A Registration Agent helps legal entities to access the network of LEI Issuers. They are usually partnered with one or more LEI Issuers. There are more than 100 Registration Agents in the Global LEI System. They must adhere to the same principles of integrity and operational ethics as other participants.

author: No author specified
content: The associated Registration Agents of LEI Issuers are listed under each LEI Issuer’s basic information. This information is accessible using the search and filter facility above.

author: No author specified
content: 3.    Validation Agents

author: No author specified
content: A variety of financial institutions and other organizations involved in identity verification and validation have elected to become Validation Agents. This enables them to obtain and maintain LEIs for their clients in cooperation with accredited LEI Issuers, thereby streamlining their client experience. Validation Agents achieve this by leveraging their business-as-usual client identification procedures in Know Your Customer (KYC), client onboarding, or standard client refresh update processes.

author: No author specified
content: Ask your financial or trust service provider if they are a Validation Agent. If so, they may be able to obtain an LEI on your behalf.

author: No author specified
content: Get a vLEI

author: No author specified
content: Once an entity has obtained a Legal Entity Identifier (LEI), it may then approach a vLEI Issuer, also referred to as Qualified vLEI Issuer (QVI) to obtain its verifiable LEI (vLEI).

author: No author specified
content: Entities can choose their vLEI Issuer based on their business needs. All vLEI Issuers have successfully completed the GLEIF Qualification Program and onboarding.

author: No author specified
content: The list below provides the vLEI Issuers’ LEI, website URL, and qualification date.

author: No author specified
content: Organizations can apply to obtain a vLEI through self-registration via a vLEI Issuer’s website.

author: No author specified
content: The fees charged for the issuance, maintenance, and revocation of vLEI Credentials are set by each vLEI Issuer.

author: No author specified
content: Become an LEI Issuer

author: No author specified
content: An organization authorized to issue Legal Entity Identifiers (LEIs) to legal entities is referred to as an LEI Issuer - also referred to as a Local Operating Unit (LOU). LEI Issuers supply registration, renewal, and other services, and act as the primary interface with legal entities for LEIs. An LEI Issuer may issue LEIs to legal entities in any jurisdiction for which it is accredited.

author: No author specified
content: Accreditation is the process by which the Global Legal Entity Identifier Foundation (GLEIF) evaluates the suitability of organizations seeking to become LEI Issuers and join the Global LEI System. Achieving GLEIF accreditation is a globally recognized designation of commitment to data quality and customer service.

author: No author specified
content: All documentation and information relevant to participation in the GLEIF accreditation process is accessible here on the GLEIF website. The six steps, which an organization seeking to become an LEI Issuer must complete, are detailed here: Accreditation Process.

author: No author specified
content: Organizations seeking assistance with their application to become a GLEIF-accredited LEI Issuer should direct their questions on the accreditation process by email to info@gleif.org.

author: No author specified
content: Accreditation Process

author: No author specified
content: The Global Legal Entity Identifier Foundation (GLEIF)  LEI Issuer, also referred to as a Local Operating Unit (LOU), accreditation process must be successfully completed by organizations seeking to become LEI Issuers. It is divided into two phases.

author: No author specified
content: The first phase requires the Applicant  LEI Issuer to create an accreditation plan. This should outline the Applicant  LEI Issuer's goals, objectives, and capabilities, and how they fit within GLEIF’s operational and control environment. After review and approval by GLEIF, the Applicant  LEI Issuer is required to sign the Master Agreement. This designates it a Candidate  LEI Issuer, and the second phase begins.

author: No author specified
content: The second phase requires the Candidate  LEI Issuer to submit the full accreditation documentation, as detailed in the Accreditation Checklist, to GLEIF for review within three months of signing the Master Agreement. GLEIF then has a review period of no longer than ninety business days to determine whether the Candidate LEI Issuer:

author: No author specified
content: Passes, receives its Accreditation Certificate, and is allowed to commence offering LEI services, or

author: No author specified
content: Fails, has its Master Agreement terminated, and is not allowed to offer LEI services, or

author: No author specified
content: Passes provisionally. In this case, GLEIF provides an explanation of the authority the Candidate Lei Issuer now has to offer LEI services and what it must do to receive the full Accreditation Certificate.

author: No author specified
content: The requirements that must be met during the accreditation process are further described in the Accreditation Manual.

author: No author specified
content: The diagram below demonstrates the six steps of the accreditation process. A summary of the actions for each step is available in the text beneath the diagram.

The requirements that must be met during the accreditation process are further described in the Accreditation Manual. The diagram below demonstrates the six steps of the accreditation process. A summary of the actions for each step is available in the text beneath the diagram.

author: No author specified
content: Applicant and Candidate Lei Issuers will receive feedback on their application and may be asked for additional information where necessary. They will then be advised of the GLEIF Board of Directors' final decision. Successful completion results in GLEIF accreditation and authorization to begin issuing LEIs to legal entities in authorized jurisdictions.

author: No author specified
content: GLEIF accreditation confirms that the Lei Issuer meets or exceeds the GLEIF quality and performance standards based upon the materials presented and testing performed; the Master Agreement requires the Lei Issuer to maintain these quality standards every operating year for an initial three-year term.

author: No author specified
content: Lifecycle of an LEI Issuer

author: No author specified
content: From the initial application through to annual verification, GLEIF outlines a clear lifecycle for an LEI Issuer, also referred to as a Local Operating Unit (LOU):

author: No author specified
imgMeta:
From the initial application through to annual verification, GLEIF outlines a clear lifecycle for an LEI Issuer, also referred to as a Local Operating Unit (LOU):   Offboarding Process The offboarding process for an LEI Issuer is triggered by any of the following three scenarios: The LEI Issuer informs GLEIF of its termination of the Master Agreement.
GLEIF informs the LEI Issuer of its termination of the Master Agreement.
The LEI Issuer is unable to conduct LEI services due to force majeure or any other significant event that prevents the LEI Issuer from operating. r any existing LEI Issuers are interested in taking over the offboarding LEI Issuer's LEI portfolio. GLEIF will then evaluate the suitability of the interested LEI Issuers and confirm which LEI Issuer(s) will take over the portfolio.

author: No author specified
content: Offboarding Process

author: No author specified
content: The offboarding process for an LEI Issuer is triggered by any of the following three scenarios:

author: No author specified
content: The LEI Issuer informs GLEIF of its termination of the Master Agreement.

author: No author specified
content: GLEIF informs the LEI Issuer of its termination of the Master Agreement.

author: No author specified
content: The LEI Issuer is unable to conduct LEI services due to force majeure or any other significant event that prevents the LEI Issuer from operating.

author: No author specified
content: Once the offboarding process is initiated, GLEIF’s ‘Trigger LEI Management Transfer’ service will start. As part of this process, GLEIF will determine whether any existing LEI Issuers are interested in taking over the offboarding LEI Issuer's LEI portfolio. GLEIF will then evaluate the suitability of the interested LEI Issuers and confirm which LEI Issuer(s) will take over the portfolio.

author: No author specified
content: The offboarding LEI Issuer, receiving LEI Issuer(s), and GLEIF will coordinate the bulk transfer of LEIs from the offboarding LEI Issuer to the receiving LEI Issuer(s).

author: No author specified
content: Upon successful completion of the bulk transfer, GLEIF will announce the offboarding of the LEI Issuer and the bulk transfer to the receiving LEI Issuer(s) on the GLEIF website.

author: No author specified
content: If the offboarding is triggered by scenario 3, GLEIF will adhere to the following process:

author: No author specified
content: In accordance with the ‘Service Level Agreement’, GLEIF shall take over the impacted LEI portfolio temporarily and trigger the service ‘Trigger LEI Management Transfer.’ This will take place within 48 hours on GLEIF’s business days, provided the impacted LEI Issuer has stopped the daily upload of LEI files.

author: No author specified
content: During this period, GLEIF will temporarily manage impacted LEIs and will announce this publicly via the GLEIF website. However, GLEIF will not act as an LEI Issuer and will not issue or renew any LEI records. GLEIF will ensure that the affected LEIs are uploaded and correctly included in the Global LEI System, and will provide contact information to facilitate individual transfer-out for the affected legal entities. If no business resumption confirmation is received from the impacted LEI Issuer until GLEIF concludes that the receiving LEI Issuer(s) have received it, GLEIF will proceed with the bulk transfer.

author: No author specified
content: The impacted LEI Issuer will be offboarded if there is no response or positive confirmation of business resumption after 3 months.

author: No author specified
content: The primary goals of the offboarding process are to ensure LEI service continuity for impacted legal entities and conduct the LEI bulk transfer in the least costly manner for the Global LEI System.

author: No author specified
content: Become a vLEI Issuer

author: No author specified
content: An organization authorized to issue verifiable LEIs (vLEIs) to legal entities, as well as the persons who represent their organizations either in official or functional roles, is referred to as a vLEI Issuer, also referred to as a Qualified vLEI Issuer (QVI).

author: No author specified
content: vLEI Issuers perform issuance, verification, and revocation of vLEI Credentials. In particular, they act as the primary interface for organizations seeking vLEI credentials. At the entity level, these are referred to as ‘Legal Entity vLEI Credentials’. Organizations holding valid Legal Entity vLEI Credentials may request the issuance of ‘Legal Entity Official Organizational Role (OOR) vLEI Credentials’ by the vLEI Issuer.

author: No author specified
content: GLEIF vLEI Issuer Qualification is the process by which the suitability of organizations seeking to become vLEI Issuers is assessed. Becoming a member of the vLEI Ecosystem via GLEIF qualification is a globally recognized designation of commitment to trusted organizational digital identity.

author: No author specified
content: vLEI Issuers will either be organizations already issuing LEIs (Local Operating Units) or new business partners. All vLEI Issuers must be qualified through the Qualification Program under the GLEIF vLEI Ecosystem Framework to be able to offer services related to vLEI Legal Entity Credentials and/or vLEI OOR Credentials.

author: No author specified
content: This section of the GLEIF website makes available all documentation and information relevant to participation in the GLEIF Qualification Program. The detailed steps, which an organization seeking to become vLEI Issuer must complete, are detailed here: Qualification Program.

author: No author specified
content: Organizations seeking assistance with their application to become a GLEIF Qualified vLEI Issuer should direct their questions on the Qualification Program by email to info@gleif.org.

author: No author specified
content: vLEI Issuer Qualification Program

author: No author specified
content: All organizations seeking to become vLEI Issuers, also referred to as Qualified vLEI Issuers (QVIs), must successfully complete the Qualification Program as established by Global Legal Entity Identifier Foundation (GLEIF), which consists of the following three steps.

author: No author specified
content: Prior to initiating the GLEIF Qualification Program, it is essential that interested organizations, i.e., Candidate vLEI Issuers, review the vLEI Issuer Qualification Program Manual and all relevant information to understand the role in the vLEI Ecosystem as well as the functions and operations of a Qualified vLEI Issuer.

author: No author specified
content: For the Qualification Program to be initiated, Candidate vLEI Issuers need to submit a Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA) signed by an authorized signatory to qualificationrequest@gleif.org.

author: No author specified
content: The next step requires the Candidate vLEI Issuer to submit the full qualification documentation, as detailed in the vLEI Issuer Qualification Program Checklist, to GLEIF for review within sixty calendar days of submitting the NDA (Please note: GLEIF has provided an online tool to be used by Candidate vLEI Issuers to complete the Qualification Checklist. The GLEIF Qualification Team will provide Candidate vLEI Issuers with the details to access the tool once a Qualification request has been submitted.) GLEIF then has ten calendar days to provide feedback on the completeness of the documentation.  Once the submission is considered complete, GLEIF has no more than 60 calendar days to assess it and provide the Candidate vLEI Issuer with the status of its application.

author: No author specified
content: Following the successful review of the qualification documentation, the Candidate vLEI Issuer is required to install the vLEI software provided by GLEIF and to test the vLEI Issuer services for vLEI Credential issuance, verification, and revocation, as well as key rotation. The timing and detailed planning of the vLEI operations testing will be agreed upon by GLEIF and the Candidate vLEI Issuer.

author: No author specified
content: Throughout the process, the Candidate vLEI Issuer will receive feedback on its application and may be asked to provide additional information where necessary. Also, upon request, GLEIF may schedule support calls with the Candidate vLEI Issuer to provide additional assistance.  The Candidate vLEI Issuer will need to remediate any issues as noted by GLEIF.

author: No author specified
content: Upon the successful completion of the above-mentioned steps, the Candidate vLEI Issuer will be notified about the final decision on its qualification application. Approval of the qualification application will result in the Candidate vLEI Issuer signing the vLEI Issuer Qualification Agreement with GLEIF. The Candidate vLEI Issuer will then become a Qualified vLEI Issuer and be authorized to begin issuing vLEI Credentials. The Qualified vLEI Issuer will also receive its  Qualified vLEI Issuer vLEI Credential and TrustMark from GLEIF.

author: No author specified
content: The GLEIF qualification confirms that the vLEI Issuer meets or exceeds the GLEIF quality and performance standards based on the materials presented and testing performed. GLEIF will reverify the vLEI Issuer’s compliance with expected internal controls and standards within vLEI operations at least on an annual basis through the Annual vLEI Issuer Qualification.

author: No author specified
content: vLEI Ecosystem Governance Framework

author: No author specified
content: The Global Legal Entity Identifier Foundation (GLEIF) has published the verifiable LEI (vLEI) Ecosystem Governance Framework based on the Trust over IP Governance Metamodel. The vLEI Ecosystem Governance Framework, designed from the ground up to complement GLEIF’s existing LEI governance, defines the vLEI operational model and describes how the new digital trust ecosystem’s range of vLEI-issuing stakeholders will qualify for and perform their roles in the Global LEI System.

author: No author specified
imgMeta: The Global Legal Entity Identifier Foundation (GLEIF) has published the verifiable LEI (vLEI) Ecosystem Governance Framework based on the Trust over IP Governance Metamodel. The vLEI Ecosystem Governance Framework, designed from the ground up to complement GLEIF’s existing LEI governance, defines the vLEI operational model and describes how the new digital trust ecosystem’s range of vLEI-issuing stakeholders will qualify for and perform their roles in the Global LEI System.   Trust over IP Governance Metamodel A year in the making, the Framework has been created in full accordance with the standards and recommendations of the Trust over IP Foundation, hosted by the Linux Foundation. It is the most comprehensive Governance Framework developed based on the ToIP Governance Framework Meta-model. s.

author: No author specified
content: A year in the making, the Framework has been created in full accordance with the standards and recommendations of the Trust over IP Foundation, hosted by the Linux Foundation. It is the most comprehensive Governance Framework developed based on the ToIP Governance Framework Meta-model.

author: No author specified
content: The Framework provides essential detail on the governance structures and processes that will shape the development of the vLEI ecosystem, together with the services that GLEIF will provide, including the vLEI Qualification Program, essential key and credential management services, and a communications platform for information sharing between GLEIF and its network of vLEI issuers.

author: No author specified
content: The documents of an Ecosystem Governance Framework comprise a Primary Document, one or more Controlled Documents (separate supporting documents focused on dedicated topics for the Framework), and the Identifier Governance and Credential Frameworks.

author: No author specified
content: Summary of Change History of vLEI Ecosystem Governance Framework

author: No author specified

author: No author specified
content:
verifiable LEI (vLEI) Ecosystem Governance Framework Primary Document
The primary document is the master Framework document. GLEIF suggests that the Primary Document is an excellent place to start when reading the Framework.

author: No author specified
content:
Controlled Documents
Documents for the vLEI Issuer Qualification Program

author: No author specified
content: There are several Controlled Documents that are dedicated to the roles and responsibilities of Qualified vLEI Issuers. These documents are part of the Legal Agreement section of the Framework. The main legal document is the vLEI Issuer Qualification Agreement, which includes seven appendices.

author: No author specified
content: The complete set of documents for vLEI Issuer Qualification can be accessed through the following link.

author: No author specified
content: Identifier Governance Frameworks and Credential Frameworks

author: No author specified
content: The Identifier Governance Framework focuses on GLEIF’s Autonomic Identifiers (AIDs) and the QVI Delegated AIDs. Each vLEI Credential has a Credential Framework for each of the five vLEI Credential types. These Frameworks include policies and requirements for the issuance, verification, and revocation of each vLEI Credential, as well as the fields contained in each Credential, with a link to the technical schema.

author: No author specified
content:
GLEIF Identifier Governance Framework
A document that details the purpose, principles, policies, and specifications that apply to the use of the GLEIF Root Autonomic Identifier (AID) and its GLEIF Delegated AIDs in the vLEI Ecosystem.

author: No author specified
content:
Qualified vLEI Issuer Identifier Governance Framework and vLEI Credential Framework
The document is the authoritative Governance Framework for the Qualified vLEI Issuer Delegated AIDs and the Credential Framework for Qualified vLEI Issuer vLEI Credential. It specifies the purpose, principles, policies, and specifications that apply to the use of the Qualified vLEI Issuer Delegated AIDs and the QVI vLEI Credential in the vLEI Ecosystem.

author: No author specified
content:
Legal Entity vLEI Credential Framework
A document that details the requirements for a vLEI Credential, the entity-level vLEI Credential issued by a Qualified vLEI Issuer to a Legal Entity.

author: No author specified
content:
Authorization vLEI Credential Framework
A document that details the requirements for the Authorization vLEI Credential which enables simple, safe, secure instruction and authorization by a Legal Entity Authorized Representative (LAR) to a Qualified vLEI Issuer (QVI)  for the issuance and revocation of Official Organizational Role vLEI Role Credentials (OOR vLEI Credentials) or Engagement Context Role vLEI Credentials (ECR vLEI Credentials), or to its LARs for the issuance and revocation of ECR vLEI Credentials directly by the Legal Entity.

author: No author specified
content:
Legal Entity Official Organizational Role vLEI Credential Framework
A document that details the requirements for vLEI Role Credentials issued by a Qualified vLEI Issuer to official representatives of a Legal Entity.

author: No author specified
content:
Legal Entity Engagement Context Role vLEI Credential Framework
A document that details the requirements for vLEI Role Credentials issued to representatives of a Legal Entity in roles other than official, but in functional or other contexts of engagement. These vLEI Role Credentials can be issued and revoked by a Legal Entity itself, or a Qualified vLEI Issuer can provide these value-added services to a Legal Entity.

Every LEI must be managed in accordance with this governance, which places GLEIF in the management role for the Global LEI System.

vLEI Issuer Qualification Agreement
An agreement between GLEIF and an organization that has been qualified by GLEIF to operate as a vLEI Issuer.  The vLEI Issuer Qualification Agreement will be the same for all vLEI Issuers.

content: Lifecycle of a vLEI Issuer

author: No author specified
content: From the initial application through to annual verification, GLEIF outlines a clear lifecycle for a vLEI Issuer, also referred to as a Qualified vLEI Issuer (QVI):

author: No author specified
imgMeta:
From the initial application through to annual verification, GLEIF outlines a clear lifecycle for a vLEI Issuer, also referred to as a Qualified vLEI Issuer (QVI):   Offboarding Process The offboarding process for a vLEI Issuer is triggered by any of the following six scenarios: The vLEI Issuer informs GLEIF of its termination of the Qualification Agreement.

GLEIF informs the vLEI Issuer of its termination of the Qualification Agreement.

The vLEI Issuer is prevented from performing its operations and duties due to force majeure or any other significant event. iated and the vLEI Issuer's legal-entity clients have been notified, GLEIF will revoke the vLEI Issuer's vLEI credential. The vLEI Issuer's clients will have a 90-day grace period to contact another vLEI Issuer and obtain new credentials.

author: No author specified
content: Offboarding Process

author: No author specified
content: The offboarding process for a vLEI Issuer is triggered by any of the following six scenarios:

author: No author specified
content: The vLEI Issuer informs GLEIF of its termination of the Qualification Agreement.

author: No author specified
content: The vLEI Issuer informs GLEIF of its termination of the Qualification Agreement.

author: No author specified
content: GLEIF informs the vLEI Issuer of its termination of the Qualification Agreement.

author: No author specified
content: GLEIF informs the vLEI Issuer of its termination of the Qualification Agreement.

author: No author specified
content: The vLEI Issuer is prevented from performing its operations and duties due to force majeure or any other significant event.

author: No author specified
content: The vLEI Issuer is prevented from performing its operations and duties due to force majeure or any other significant event.

author: No author specified
content: Once the offboarding process is initiated and the vLEI Issuer's legal-entity clients have been notified, GLEIF will revoke the vLEI Issuer's vLEI credential. The vLEI Issuer's clients will have a 90-day grace period to contact another vLEI Issuer and obtain new credentials.

author: No author specified
content: GLEIF's goal is to ensure a smooth transition of vLEI services from the terminated vLEI Issuer to a new vLEI Issuer, and it will assess communications and other requirements on a case-by-case basis.

author: No author specified
content: Registration Agents

author: No author specified
content: Registration Agents help legal entities access the network of LEI Issuers, also referred to as a Local Operating Unit (LOUs). More than 100 organizations operate as Registration Agents in the Global LEI System, contributing to the growing rate of LEI adoption worldwide.

author: No author specified
content: A Registration Agent may choose to partner with one or more LEI Issuers to ensure its clients’ needs for LEI services are met. They must adhere to the same principles of integrity and operational ethics as all other participants in the Global LEI System.

author: No author specified
content: Registration Agents are listed under their corresponding LEI Issuer’s basic information, accessible via the LEI Issuer search and filter facility here.

author: No author specified
content: The duties of Registration Agents are specified in the Appendix 6 to the Master Agreement. The full duties of an LEI Issuer are also specified in the Service Level Agreement, i.e., Appendix 6 to the Master Agreement. Moreover, Appendix 14 to the Master Agreement specifies the requirements for Third Party services, including Registration Agents. The Master Agreement is the contractual framework governing the relationship between GLEIF and LEI Issuers. It will always be the LEI Issuer that remains fully responsible and liable to GLEIF for the execution of its duties.

author: No author specified
content: LEI Issuers, in partnership with Registration Agents, must ensure that all guidelines specified in the Registration Agents Governance Framework are followed. Non-compliance with the guidelines outlined in the Registration Agents Governance Framework may result in formal complaints. The Complaint Management Process and the Complaint Form are included in the Registration Agents Governance Framework and are available to the public.

author: No author specified
content: For more information about Registration Agents, contact info@gleif.org.

author: No author specified
content: Why the LEI?

author: No author specified
content: The LEI enables clear and unique identification of legal entities participating in any transaction globally. It does this by connecting to an entity’s key reference information, which GLEIF makes publicly accessible via its website. The LEI facilitates surveillance activities like Know-Your-Customer (KYC) and Anti-Money Laundering (AML) processes. It is widely mandated and used to ensure counterparty transparency across many sectors, including financial services, supply chain, trade finance and payments.

author: No author specified
content: Better Knowledge, Better Business

author: No author specified
content: The way we work has been revolutionized by technology. The automation and digitization of many manual processes has led to considerable efficiencies.  Yet many organizations continue to use manual methods when it comes to legal entity identification, at great expense in terms of time and cost. The Legal Entity Identifier (LEI) enables a smarter, less costly and more reliable way to make decisions about who to do business with. Importantly, it is relevant across a wide range of business use cases, spanning multiple industries, business activities and functions.

author: No author specified
content: As a globally recognized identifier, the LEI enables clear and unique identification of legal entities participating in any transactions, including financial and digital exchanges, by connecting to key reference information made publicly accessible on GLEIF’s website. This central repository of LEI records is called the Global LEI Index and it is the only global online resource that provides open, standardized and high quality legal entity reference data. Each LEI contains information about an entity’s ownership structure, answering the questions of ‘who is who’ and ‘who owns whom’.

author: No author specified
content: The Global LEI Index empowers organizations, regardless of sector, to cut costs, simplify and accelerate operations and gain deeper insight into the global marketplace. Access to and usage of the LEI data pool supports a multitude of applications in, for example, risk management, compliance, and client relationship management.

author: No author specified
content: The benefits that the Global LEI Index generates for the wider business community grow in line with the rate of LEI adoption. So, GLEIF’s message to businesses around the globe is this: Get an LEI and make it work for you.

content: Featuring the LEI

author: No author specified
content: While the origin of the LEI is firmly rooted in financial services and regulation, its future undeniably points to universal applicability across public and private sectors, with relevance to organizations large and small. Regulations, recommendations, and evolving business cases across the globe present an opportunity for additional emerging sectors and ecosystems to benefit from the LEI and to see an immediate and positive impact.

author: No author specified
content: In the fight against money laundering, terrorism financing, and other forms of financial crime, more than 200 financial regulators worldwide have to date mandated the LEI among legal entities engaging in capital markets. But GLEIF’s ambition for the LEI does not stop there. The Global LEI System is continuing to expand beyond regulated use and re-focusing to help organizations harness the LEI’s capability to bring greater trust, efficiencies, and transparency to trade of all kinds. Most notably:

author: No author specified
content: A trusted and reliable method of identifying legal entities within digital environments is a vital prerequisite to secure, seamless, and well-functioning global value chains. The Global LEI System’s root of trust, which bridges national boundaries, is the organizational identity solution that will enable digital trade.

author: No author specified
content: The LEI’s unique value propositions - machine readable, open, and easily integrated– means it can be used to reliably identify counterparties in milliseconds. The vLEI (verifiable LEI), the secure digital counterpart of an LEI, provides the trust and verifiability needed to create a verifiable supply chain. The vLEI provides an authentic digital identity to all businesses and persons acting on behalf of the businesses along the supply chain.

author: No author specified
content: The Global LEI System supports the solid foundations needed for an efficient and successful global trading environment. It mitigates the risk of fraud and money laundering while supporting the financial inclusion of small and medium companies, helping to create a sustainable and responsible business environment.

author: No author specified
content: Building a global climate information architecture requires a universal way to identify and authenticate the legal entities involved. Efforts to standardize ESG data continue to evolve, with industry experts highlighting that those identifiers that remain consistent and enable interoperability will be vital tools when seamlessly connecting financial and nonfinancial datasets with existing data infrastructures. Firms will be able to gain valuable insights much faster by adopting standard universal and persistent identifier rather than non-standard identifiers and datasets. By tagging entities with the LEI and using it as an ESG data connector, transparency can be increased for the reporting entity, related companies, and even for suppliers. Inclusion of the LEI in the due diligence checklist by ESG ratings and data products providers can help to assess the information and screen inconsistencies during the pre-validation phase and contribute to investors, informing their investment decisions.

author: No author specified
content: Publicly available, high-quality, and electronically accessible company data is the foundation upon which we can combat illicit finance and address hidden risks in financial markets and global supply chains, as well as the criminal use of companies, particularly as business operations become more automated. The Financial Action Task Force (FATF) has identified data-sharing and data standardization as key approaches to addressing the lack of transparency. Together, these enable the advanced analytics needed to support effective anti-money laundering (AML) and counter-terrorist financing (CFT) initiatives across borders. Automating the process of identifying an entity’s legal form can lower risk, create transparency, and increase operational efficiency by enabling straight-through processing (STP) capabilities.

content: LEI in Cross-Border Payments

author: No author specified
content: The LEI enables cross-border payment transactions to become faster, cheaper, more transparent, and more inclusive.

author: No author specified
imgMeta:
The LEI enables cross-border payment transactions to become faster, cheaper, more transparent, and more inclusive.   *Use cases are based on the Payment Market Practice Group White Paper: Global Adoption of the LEI in ISO20022 Payment Messages – 2021 cross-border payments. This includes the definition and harmonization of data fields - including identifiers - being transmitted along the payment chain. 
 
 GLEIF is working with leading stakeholders in the payments industry to demonstrate the significant value that the LEI brings to non-financial corporates and financial institutions when used in cross-border payment flows. The five key use cases are: screening; KYC and client onboarding; fraud detection and fight against vendor scams; e-invoice reconciliation; and account-to-account validation.

author: No author specified
content:
When the LEI is added as a data attribute in payment messages, any originator or beneficiary legal entity can be precisely, instantly, and automatically identified across borders. As payment market infrastructures worldwide move to support instant payments, the ability to verify and validate the originator and beneficiary of a transaction in near real time is a foundational requirement to enable consumers, businesses, and financial institutions to confirm that funds are transferred across international borders to the correct entity.
 
The Financial Stability Board (FSB) has endorsed the LEI for supporting the goals of its G20-endorsed Roadmap for Enhancing Cross-Border Payments. As part of this initiative, and in collaboration with other industry standard-setting bodies, the FSB is currently working to promote standardized use in ISO 20022 payments messaging to mitigate constraints in cross-border payments. This includes the definition and harmonization of data fields - including identifiers - being transmitted along the payment chain. 
 
 GLEIF is working with leading stakeholders in the payments industry to demonstrate the significant value that the LEI brings to non-financial corporates and financial institutions when used in cross-border payment flows. The five key use cases are: screening; KYC and client onboarding; fraud detection and fight against vendor scams; e-invoice reconciliation; and account-to-account validation.

content: Global Value Chains

author: No author specified
content: A trusted and reliable method of identifying legal entities within digital environments is a vital prerequisite to secure, seamless, and well-functioning global value chains.

author: No author specified
imgMeta: A trusted and reliable method of identifying legal entities within digital environments is a vital prerequisite to secure, seamless, and well-functioning global value chains.   international trade, bringing both opportunities and challenges. Disruptions like the COVID-19 pandemic and geopolitical events have highlighted issues such as fragmented supplier verification processes, lack of transparency, and operational inefficiencies. These problems can disrupt the flow of goods and erode trust between partners. However, as noted in the International Chamber of Commerce (ICC) UK – Scaling the Use of Digital Identities in Trade report, improving information and transaction processes in international trade could save between $20 billion to $100 billion by reducing information and transaction costs.

author: No author specified
content: The digital revolution has transformed international trade, bringing both opportunities and challenges. Disruptions like the COVID-19 pandemic and geopolitical events have highlighted issues such as fragmented supplier verification processes, lack of transparency, and operational inefficiencies. These problems can disrupt the flow of goods and erode trust between partners. However, as noted in the International Chamber of Commerce (ICC) UK – Scaling the Use of Digital Identities in Trade report, improving information and transaction processes in international trade could save between $20 billion to $100 billion by reducing information and transaction costs.

author: No author specified
content: The ICC Digital Standards Initiative, in its KTDDE report, recommends LEIs be utilized in the digital systems of exporters, importers, financial institutions, and logistics providers. This will help develop digital platforms that work with many major standards. The report also demonstrates just how vital entity identification is – 25 percent of the key data elements across 36 core trade documents are all associated with parties involved with trade. The LEI and vLEI are the foundational enabler for trade digitalization by offering the only globally interoperable, platform agnostic organizational identity solution.

author: No author specified
content: The vLEI will change trade by enabling authentic data transfer. This innovative credential, used in digital wallets, adheres to the "never trust, always verify" principle, adding trust to supply chain organizations. For tasks like onboarding, trade finance, and customs filings, data between parties can be signed and verified using a global protocol. The vLEI's Authentic Chained Data Container (ACDC) specification ensures transactions can be securely linked.

author: No author specified
content: Businesses are now building trust programs to manage digital identities. Using LEIs and vLEIs, businesses can significantly improve global supply chains, make informed decisions, reduce financial and reputational risks, and build a strong foundation for Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) efforts. Companies have now the opportunity to leverage international standards like the LEI to unambiguously identify the parties involved in global trade transactions and their electronic trade documents like eBoL or invoicing. As noted in the “Scaling the use of digital identities in trade” report, “the LEI is one point to locate among tons of data, irrespective of the geographical location of the clients.”

content: LEI in Sustainability Reporting and  Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG)
pageTitle: LEI in Sustainability Reporting and  Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG)

author: No author specified
content: Building a global climate information architecture requires a universal way to identify and authenticate the legal entities involved. Efforts to standardize ESG data continue to evolve, with industry experts highlighting that those identifiers that remain consistent and enable interoperability will be vital tools when seamlessly connecting emerging ESG datasets with existing data infrastructures. Success in this regard will allow firms to gain valuable insights much faster than their attempts to integrate non-standard identifiers and datasets. To analyze a company’s performance across ESG factors, for example, investors need to unambiguously identify the entities engaged in activities that, for example, produce greenhouse gas emissions, to be able to analyze and understand the climate-related impact.
pageTitle: LEI in Sustainability Reporting and  Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG)

author: No author specified
content: The identification of legal entities in a unique and unambiguous manner is crucial to identify (i) physical risk, (ii) transition risk and (iii) liability risk. This was underlined in the Network for Greening Financial System (NGFS) Progress Report, authored by a network of 83 central banks and financial supervisors, which highlights that a major obstacle to accessing and making use of existing climate-related data is the lack of unique identifiers which are crucial for interlinking climate-related data and financial data. The LEI can help support this initiative by linking financial and non-financial information. When legal entities, such as investment funds and government entities, together with corporate events, are defined and tracked using different identifiers, it can be extremely challenging for investors and/or regulators to analyze the ESG performance for a given entity over time as each tracker needs to be reconciled and mapped back to the underlying entity.
pageTitle: LEI in Sustainability Reporting and  Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG)

author: No author specified
content: Leveraging the LEI can help to assess counterparty activites, emissions and the resulting risks along the value chains across countries and aggregated data.   Being able to identify the activities of their counterparties unambiguously and persistently also will enable companies to analyze and assess their progress toward their committed climate-related and environmental targets.
pageTitle: LEI in Sustainability Reporting and  Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG)

author: No author specified
content: The LEI will enable cross-border coordination and cooperation as authorities develop modalities, datasets and reporting frameworks to exchange with each other given the global nature of climate-related risks. With the LEI, supervisory authorities can identify financial institutions’ exposure to economic activities impacted by transition risk. The LEI can also provide detail regarding the geographical location of financial institutions’ exposures most prone to physical risk. Moreover, the LEI can facilitate data collection of financial institutions with regards to counterparties/borrowers. The Financial Stability Board (FSB) recommends adding the LEI of financial institutions’ counterparties in data reporting templates to help increase the reliability of climate-related data used and reported by financial institutions.
pageTitle: LEI in Sustainability Reporting and  Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG)

author: No author specified
content: The European System Risk Board in its recommendation highlighted that the LEI can support the data collection on sustainable finance. “A central harmonised database of relevant information on each company’s degree of sustainability and its exposures to climate risks would be beneficial for supporting the development of sustainable finance and ensuring investor protection by enabling easy access to the financial and environmental, social and governance (ESG) metrics describing the company. Such data would enable supply chains to be tracked (e.g. through the LEIs of suppliers and clients of companies). This would make it possible to estimate emissions across entire supply chains. In turn, it would help monitor the use of the proceeds of green bonds, make green labels more reliable and thus lower the reputational risk of “greenwashing” in green bonds markets.”
pageTitle: LEI in Sustainability Reporting and  Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG)

author: No author specified
content: The LEI is already required, where available, under the final EU Regulatory Technical Standards (RTS) under the Sustainable Finance Disclosure Regulation (SFDR) . In China, the LEI is required based on the Guidelines for Environmental Information Disclosure of Financial Institutions in Shenzhen.
pageTitle: LEI in Sustainability Reporting and  Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG)

author: No author specified
content: Some data vendors have already incorporated the LEI into their databases. For example, ESGi, a provider of trusted ESG data for the investment industry, has embedded LEIs into its platform, taking advantage of the International Securities Identification Number (ISIN) to LEI link initiated by GLEIF and helping buy-side firms identify which companies products are exposed to.
pageTitle: LEI in Sustainability Reporting and  Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG)

author: No author specified
content: In December 2022, GLEIF joined the Net Zero initiative. The value of the LEI has been immediately recognized, given the lack of a mandated, global common identifier in the corporate world creates many challenges to linking company records solely on non-standardized, country-specific ID’s or cumbersome textual data. Truman Semans, CEO and Founder of OS-Climate, said “In the context of our initiative, matching data using GLEIF’s LEI and other open datasets is a key element in building high quality analytics systems that assess companies' progress relative to their commitments to Net Zero and other climate-aligned investment goals.” Alongside this work GLEIF is the co-champion of the ‘discoverable data’ subgroup for The Carbon Call, helping promote transparency in sustainability reporting to support The Carbon Call roadmap released at COP27.
pageTitle: LEI in Sustainability Reporting and  Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG)

author: No author specified
content: The value of the LEI in supporting the G20’s sustainability agenda has recently been recognized in a report published by leading global business community influencers (the B20, Business in OECD and IoE). The LEI is recognized for its capacity to reduce costs and fragmented approaches across borders for the business community and help set the stage for better risk management information in the future.
pageTitle: LEI in Sustainability Reporting and  Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG)
